Version.Name: Fish Acute (LC50) Toxicity model (IRFMN)
Version.Summary: Quantitative model for acute toxicity (LC50) in fish.

Guide.Description: The Fish Acute (LC50 96h) toxicity model (IRFMN) –v.1.0.0 is based on 331 experimental data on Oryzias latipes retrieved from Japanese Ministry of Environment (http://www.env.go.jp/en/chemi/sesaku/aquatic_Mar_2016.pdf) and selected according to the OECD TG 203 requirements. The model is a Tree Ensemble Random Forest. This model has been trained for the end points LC50 96h, acute (mortality).
Guide.Dataset: The model is based on Fish Acute (LC50 96h) toxicity of 331 experimental data on Oryzias latipes selected according to OECD TG 203 requirements. Data are continuous experimental toxicity values from the Ministry of Environment in Japan. Data are freely available, since March 2016, at the link https://www.env.go.jp/en/chemi/sesaku/aquatic_Mar_2016.pdf. The experimental values came from aquatic toxicity tests performed according to OECD-GLP standards and the OECD official guidelines. Pre-processing of data before modelling SMILES creation and neutralization: Firstly, we generated the SMILES structures from the chemical name and CAS RN for each substance using ChemCell (2019) and Marvin View (Marvin 17.28.0, 2012017, ChemAxon, 2019). We manually checked the correspondence and correctness among the obtained structures, chemical name and CAS RN among several websites and public database like ChemIDplus Advanced ( NIH, 2019), PubChem (NCBI, 2019), ChemSpider (Royal Society of Chemistry, 2019), DSSTox. Then, we added several structures, which have not automatically generated. We normalized the SMILES with istMolBase 1.0.3. (in-house software), then we neutralized them using KNIME 3.5. Since pH is a critical issue in the experimental assays on algae, we considered ionized normalized SMILES and we calculated the major microspecies at pH 7.5 and 8.1 using JChem for Excel. We removed the compounds for which the SMILES changed depending on pH (in range 7.5-8.1). Cleaning of the structure: We cleaned the datasets excluding the following compounds: metal complexes inorganic mixtures of structural isomers ambiguous structures non-ionic surfactant mixtures complex disconnected structures (e.g. polymers) chemicals whose correspondence name-CAS was not found UVCBsalts; only the acid form was kept. Values cleaning: We selected continuous experimental values excluding those reported as a range, greater/less than a certain threshold, or approximate values. We converted each experimental value from mg/l to mmol/l, on the basis of the molecular weight calculated from the chemical structure. We also removed the compounds for which the experimental toxicity values were higher than the experimental water solubility values. For this pourpose we retrieved the experimental water solubility values mainly from a large database of more than 4,000 chemicals that we pruned in the LIFE project ANTARES and from Guide Chem and Sigma-Aldrich websites in the case we did not find the water solubilities elsewhere. Dealing with multiple values: To deal with multiple continuous data we referred to the procedures described in ECHA guidance R.10 (2008) for ecotoxicological continuous endpoints. In case the experimental conditions and the reliability of the studies were the same, we considered the ratio between the maximum and the minimum values; if it was higher than one log unit we eliminated the data. Then, we calculated the median, the arithmetic and geometric mean in mmol/l to check if there were differences among them. We found a very good correlation (R2) between the values of each combination (arithmetic vs geometric mean, arithmetic mean vs median, geometric mean vs median) and finally the geometric mean was preferred (ECHA guidance R.10, 2008). To normalize the data we performed two types of transformation, the logarithm of the geometric mean and the Box-cox transformation. Since the box-cox transformation gave better results in terms of normalization of the data, it was finally used to normalize the data. We excluded data falling outside the range (mean of the box-cox transformed values) ± 3*(standard deviation). Concerning the final chronic fish toxicity, once we merged all the dataset, the final dataset increased up to 94 chemicals. Availability of the training set: Yes Data for the dependent variable for the training set: All Availability of the external validation set: No Data for the dependent variable for the external validation set: All Other information about the training set: To derive the model, the data have been divided in training and test sets with the ratio of 80:20. In order to obtain a uniform distribution of the endpoint values between the two subsets an activity and descriptors sampling method have been applied. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was performed on all the descriptors and to select the first two principal components. Five random compounds have been selected, and then the most dissimilar compound have been picked from the sample pool according to the first two principal components and the response using several combinations of distance metrics and scoring functions. Then the compound was added to the pool repeating the operation until the desired number for the training set is reached. Other information about the external validation set: NA
Guide.Descriptors: Type of model: The Fish Acute (LC50 96h) toxicity model (IRFMN) –v.1.0.0 is based on 331 experimental data on Oryzias latipes retrieved from Japanese Ministry of Environment (http://www.env.go.jp/en/chemi/sesaku/aquatic_Mar_2016.pdf), and selected according to the OECD TG 203 requirements. The model is a Tree Ensemble Random Forest. Explicit algorithm: Tree Ensemble Random Forest Among the several algorithms used, the best results obtained in terms of performance has been with a Random Forest called Tree ensemble. Tree ensemble builds a series of regression trees with different rows and different variables (according to certain parameters) and then it aggregates the results as an ensemble of models. It chooses the parameters for the variables of each tree and the number of compounds evaluating the performance of several models (Hyperparameter tuning Research) using as metric R2 of a Bootstrap (100 iterations) cross-validation on training set. Algorithm and descriptor generation: To reduce the great number of variables, all the descriptors with constant values (var(X) =0), or which correlate over 0.95 (Pearson) with at least one another descriptor have been removed. In order to select the variables two methods implemented in R packages for each dataset have been used: the genetic algorithm (gaselect package) and the Variable Selection Using Random Forest (VSURF) package. Both the pools of variables of each dataset have been imported into a KNIME workflow to derive the models. Software name and version for descriptor generation: DRAGON Calculation of several sets of molecular descriptors from molecular geometries (topological, geometrical, WHIM, 3D-MoRSE, molecular profiles, etc.) Prof. R.Todeschini - distributed by Talete srl, via Pisani 13, 20124 Milano, Italy http://www.disat.unimib.it/chm Descriptors in the model: Dragon 7.0 extension for KNIME 2 D descriptors Descriptor selection: The descriptors have been calculated using Dragon 7.0 extension for KNIME to calculate the descriptors, resulting in 3839 2D descriptors. Then a pruning process both to the compounds and to the descriptors pools was applied. Firstly, the compounds for which it was not feasible to calculate AlogP (Ghose-Crippen octanol-water partition coefficient (Ghose and Crippen, 1986; Viswanadhan et al., 1993; Ghose et al., 1998)) have been removed, as it is generally well acknowledged that this descriptor is the most correlated to the response.

AD.Name: Similar_Molecules
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: strongly similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: only moderately similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: no similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found

AD.Name: Accuracy
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is good
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not optimal
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not adequate

AD.Name: Concordance
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that agree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that slightly disagree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that completely disagree with the target compound predicted value

AD.Name: Maximum_Error
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a low value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a moderate value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a high value, considering the experimental variability

AD.Name: Model_Descriptors_Range_Check
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: descriptors for this compound have values inside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: descriptors for this compound have values outside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set

AD.Name: ACF_SimCheck
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: all atom centered fragment of the compound have been found in the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: some atom centered fragment of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: a prominent number of atom centered fragments of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments

AD.Name: AD_Index
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: predicted substance is into the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: predicted substance could be out of the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: predicted substance is out of the the Applicability Domain of the model

xml.Reference: For a general description of the VEGA software see: Benfenati E, Manganaro A, Gini G VEGA-QSAR: AI inside a platform for predictive toxicology Proceedings of the workshop "Popularize Artificial Intelligence 2013", December 5th 2013, Turin, Italy Published on CEUR Workshop Proceedings Vol-1107
xml.Endpoint: The endpoint is the short-term toxicity to fish according to ECOTOX 6.1.1 (LC50 96h, acute (mortality)). The specie is Fish (Oryzias latipes)
